title: Exponentials & Logarithms
blurb: Growth that multiplies instead of adds, the logarithm as its reverse gear, three laws that turn products into sums, and the equations behind interest, populations, and half-lives.
buildsOn: Unit 5's rational exponents and inverse functions.

lessons.title: Exponential Growth & Decay
lessons.concreteComparison: Fold a sheet of paper 42 times and its thickness would reach the Moon. Nothing in daily life prepares intuition for repeated DOUBLING — linear thinking undershoots exponential reality catastrophically. This lesson is the antidote.
lessons.objective: Model repeated-multiplication situations with f(x) = a·bˣ, distinguish growth from decay, and read a, b from stories and graphs.
concept: $f(x) = a \cdot b^x$: start value $a$ (the $y$-intercept), factor $b$ per step. $b > 1$: **growth** ($b = 1.05$ is +5% per step); $0 < b < 1$: **decay** ($b = 0.8$ is −20% per step). Percent language: $b = 1 + r$.

Exponential vs linear is MULTIPLY vs ADD: linear $+300$/year vs exponential $\times 1.05$/year. Any constant-percent story — interest, population, depreciation, half-life — is exponential by definition.

Graph anatomy: passes through $(0, a)$; climbs (or dives) by factor $b$ each unit step; hugs the x-axis on its shrinking side (horizontal asymptote $y = 0$) but NEVER touches: $a \cdot b^x$ has no zeros.
lessons.keyIdea: f(x) = a·bˣ multiplies by b each step: b = 1 + r encodes percent change, growth above 1, decay below, asymptote at zero, never touching.

facts.title: The model
facts.explanation: $a$: start; $b$: per-step factor; $r$: percent rate as a decimal.

facts.title: Growth vs decay
facts.explanation: Both hug zero on one side and explode on the other.

workedExamples.statement: A town of 20,000 grows 5% per year. Write the model and find the population after 3 years.
workedExamples.solution: $P(t) = 20000(1.05)^t$. $P(3) = 20000(1.157625) = 23152.5$ — about 23,153 people. (Linear +1000/yr would give 23,000: the gap is compounding, and it widens every year.)

workedExamples.statement: A car worth $24{,}000 loses 15% of its value each year. Model it and find the value after 2 years.

commonMistakes.correction: $b$ is the KEEP factor, not the change: 100% kept plus 5% gained → $b = 1.05$. ($b = 0.05$ would erase 95% of the town yearly.)

commonMistakes.text: Modeling 15% decay as subtracting 15% of the ORIGINAL each year (linear).
commonMistakes.correction: Each year takes 15% of the CURRENT value: multiply by 0.85 repeatedly. Year 2 loses less than year 1 in dollars — decay curves flatten, lines don't.

tryIt.statement: A colony of 500 bacteria doubles every hour. Write the model and count bacteria after 6 hours.
Which grows larger by year 10: $\$1000$ at $+8\%$ per year, or $\$1000$ plus $\$90$ per year?
tryIt.solution: Exponential: $1000(1.08)^{10} \approx 2158.9$. Linear: $1000 + 900 = 1900$. The 8% curve wins — and the gap keeps widening forever after.

steps.title: Multiplying, not adding
steps.body: Interest earns interest. Offspring have offspring. Each year's change is a PERCENT of the current amount — so bigger amounts change faster. Lines (constant addition) can't tell this story; a new function family can. Meet $f(x) = a \cdot b^x$: the mathematics of compounding.

steps.title: Two dials: a and b
steps.teach: Step the start value $a$ and the base $b$. The ×b chips along the curve are the signature: EVERY step right multiplies by the same factor. Push $b$ below 1 and growth flips to decay — same machine, shrinking. Notice the curve hugging the axis on one side: the asymptote.

steps.title: From percent to base
steps.body: Stories speak percent; the model wants a factor. Convert with $b = 1 + r$: '+5%' → $b = 1.05$; '−15%' → $b = 0.85$; 'doubles' → $b = 2$; 'half-life' → $b = \frac{1}{2}$ per half-life period. The factor answers: of each unit, how much exists one step later?

steps.title: Percent to base
steps.prompt: A quantity shrinks 7% per day. Its daily factor $b$ is:
steps.explanation: Keep $100\% - 7\% = 93\%$: $b = 0.93$. The factor is what REMAINS, not what leaves.

steps.title: Curve vs line: the race
steps.teach: An exponential racing a straight line. Early on the line can lead — watch closely — but the curve's growth GROWS, and once it passes the line it never looks back. Every exponential eventually beats every line; 'eventually' is the operative word intuition misses.

steps.title: The doubling pond
steps.prompt: Lily pads double daily and cover the pond on day 30. When was the pond HALF covered?
steps.explanation: One doubling from full: day 29. Half the time (day 15) covers just $\frac{1}{2^{15}}$ — about three-millionths. Exponentials back-load everything.

steps.title: Name a, b, and the step
steps.body: Before computing, write three labels: start $a$, factor $b$, and what one STEP means (a year? an hour? a half-life?). Most exponential errors are unit errors — a monthly rate raised to years, a half-life treated as a day. The three-label header prevents them all.

steps.title: What sticks
points: $b > 1$ grows, $0 < b < 1$ decays; asymptote $y = 0$, no zeros.
Exponentials multiply; lines add — and the curve always wins eventually.

lessons.title: Logarithms & Their Meaning
lessons.concreteComparison: "2 to the what gives 32?" Your brain answers 5 by silently counting doublings. The logarithm is that silent count given a symbol: log₂32 = 5. It's not a new creature — it's the exponential machine from last lesson, running backwards.
lessons.objective: Convert between exponential and logarithmic form, evaluate logs mentally, and graph log functions as reflected exponentials.
concept: **Definition**: $\log_b x = y$ means $b^y = x$ — the log answers 'what exponent?'. $\log_2 32 = 5$ since $2^5 = 32$; $\log_{10} 1000 = 3$; $\log_5 \frac{1}{25} = -2$ since $5^{-2} = \frac{1}{25}$.

Instant anchors from the definition: $\log_b 1 = 0$ ($b^0 = 1$), $\log_b b = 1$, and $\log_b b^k = k$. Only POSITIVE numbers have logs: $b^y$ never outputs 0 or negatives, so $\log_b 0$ and $\log_b(-8)$ don't exist.

$y = \log_b x$ is the INVERSE (Unit 5!) of $y = b^x$: graphs mirror across $y = x$; domain and range swap. The log graph lives right of the y-axis (vertical asymptote $x = 0$), crosses $(1, 0)$, and climbs forever — but agonizingly slowly.
lessons.keyIdea: log_b x is the exponent b needs to reach x — the exponential's inverse: log of 1 is 0, only positives allowed, graph mirrored across y = x with a wall at x = 0.

facts.title: The two forms
facts.explanation: One fact, two costumes — translate freely between them.

workedExamples.solution: $2^5 = 32$: answer 5. $9^{1/2} = 3$: answer $\frac{1}{2}$. $4^{-2} = \frac{1}{16}$: answer $-2$. Every log question is an exponent question in costume.

workedExamples.statement: Convert $5^3 = 125$ to log form and $\log_7 x = 2$ to exponential form; solve the latter.

commonMistakes.correction: The log asks for an EXPONENT, not a quotient: $2^3 = 8$, so $\log_2 8 = 3$. When in doubt, write the exponential costume.
$3^{-2} = \frac{1}{9}$ — positive, like every power of 3. No exponent produces $-9$: logs of negatives (and of zero) do not exist.

steps.title: The backwards question
steps.body: Last lesson: given time, compute the amount — $500 \cdot 2^t$ at $t = 6$. Real life asks the reverse: WHEN does the colony hit a million? The unknown is upstairs, in the exponent, and no tool you own reaches up there. The reach tool is the logarithm — and Unit 5 told you exactly what it must be: the exponential's inverse.

steps.title: An exponent, named
steps.body: $\log_b x$ = the exponent that $b$ needs to become $x$. That's the entire definition: $\log_2 32 = 5$ because $2^5 = 32$. Two costumes, one fact: $\log_b x = y \iff b^y = x$. Fluency = translating between costumes without friction, both directions.

steps.title: What exponent?
steps.explanation: $6^3 = 216$. The log IS the exponent — 3, a small number, not a division result.

steps.title: Reading the curve backwards
steps.teach: The race view answers log questions graphically: 'when does $2^x$ reach 20?' is asking WHERE the curve meets that height — read DOWN from the crossing to the x-axis, and that x is $\log_2 20$ (a bit over 4.3). Evaluating a log = reading an exponential graph sideways.

steps.title: Freebies
steps.explanation: $13^0 = 1$ — every base reaches 1 with exponent 0. $\log_b 1 = 0$ is universal, like $b^0 = 1$ wearing the other costume.

steps.title: Mirror across the diagonal
steps.body: As the inverse of $y = b^x$, the log graph is its mirror across $y = x$ (Unit 5's picture). Everything swaps: the exponential's horizontal asymptote becomes the log's VERTICAL wall at $x = 0$; the y-intercept $(0,1)$ becomes the x-intercept $(1, 0)$; 'positive outputs only' becomes 'positive INPUTS only.' And the log climbs forever — just heartbreakingly slowly: $\log_2$ of a million is still under 20.

steps.title: When stuck, change costumes
steps.body: Every confusing log statement clarifies as an exponential, and vice versa. $\log_x 64 = 3$ feels alien; $x^3 = 64$ is a Tuesday. Make the translation your first move, not your last resort.

steps.title: The calculators of 1614
steps.body: Napier published logarithms as a computational hack: they turn multiplication into addition (next lesson's laws), so 17th-century astronomers multiplied giant numbers by ADDING their logs from tables. Slide rules mechanized the trick and ran engineering for 350 years — including the Apollo program's backup calculations.

steps.title: What sticks
points: $\log_b x = y \iff b^y = x$: the log IS the exponent.
Graph: exponential mirrored across $y = x$; wall at $x = 0$; slow climb.

lessons.title: Properties of Logarithms
lessons.concreteComparison: Exponents add when powers multiply: b^m · b^n = b^(m+n). Logs ARE exponents — so logs of products must add. Three exponent laws, wearing log costumes: that's the entire lesson, and it's the engine that will crack every exponential equation.
lessons.objective: Apply the product, quotient, and power rules to expand and condense log expressions, and use the change-of-base formula.
concept: Three laws, inherited straight from exponents: **product** $\log_b(MN) = \log_b M + \log_b N$; **quotient** $\log_b \frac{M}{N} = \log_b M - \log_b N$; **power** $\log_b(M^k) = k \log_b M$ — the exponent-puller, star of the show.

**Expand** to split composites: $\log_2 \frac{8x^3}{y} = 3 + 3\log_2 x - \log_2 y$. **Condense** to a single log: $2\log_5 x + \log_5 3 = \log_5(3x^2)$ — power rule first (coefficients become exponents), then product/quotient.

**Change of base**: $\log_b x = \frac{\log_c x}{\log_c b}$ for any base $c$ — how a base-10 calculator computes $\log_7 50$, and a genuinely useful identity: $\log_8 32 = \frac{\log_2 32}{\log_2 8} = \frac{5}{3}$.
lessons.keyIdea: Logs are exponents, so products add, quotients subtract, and powers pull out front; change of base rewrites any log as a ratio in a friendlier base.

facts.title: The three laws
facts.latex: \log MN = \log M + \log N; \;\; \log \tfrac{M}{N} = \log M - \log N; \;\; \log M^k = k\log M
facts.explanation: Product, quotient, power — same base throughout.

facts.title: Change of base
facts.explanation: New base $c$, your choice — 10, $e$, or whatever's convenient.

workedExamples.solution: Quotient first: $\log_2(8x^3) - \log_2 y$. Product: $\log_2 8 + \log_2 x^3 - \log_2 y$. Power + anchor: $3 + 3\log_2 x - \log_2 y$.

workedExamples.statement: Condense $2\log_5 x + \log_5 3 - \log_5 y$ into one logarithm, and evaluate $\log_8 32$ by change of base.
workedExamples.solution: Power rule: $\log_5 x^2$; then product and quotient: $\log_5 \frac{3x^2}{y}$. Change of base through 2: $\log_8 32 = \frac{\log_2 32}{\log_2 8} = \frac{5}{3}$.

commonMistakes.text: Splitting the log of a SUM: $\log(x + y) = \log x + \log y$.
commonMistakes.correction: The product rule needs a PRODUCT inside: $\log(xy) = \log x + \log y$. Sums inside logs don't split — there is no law for them.

commonMistakes.text: Turning $\frac{\log M}{\log N}$ into $\log M - \log N$.
commonMistakes.correction: The quotient rule is about $\log\frac{M}{N}$ — quotient INSIDE one log. A ratio OF two logs is change-of-base material and simplifies differently ($\frac{\log 32}{\log 8} = \frac{5}{3}$, not $\log 4$).

steps.title: Taming composite insides
steps.body: Real formulas stuff products, quotients, and powers inside logs: $\log \frac{8x^3}{y}$. To solve equations (next lesson) you need to take such things apart and put them back together. The disassembly manual is three laws — and you already know them, because you know exponent laws.

steps.title: Same laws, log costume
steps.body: $b^m \cdot b^n = b^{m+n}$: multiply powers, ADD exponents. Since logs ARE exponents, take logs of that sentence: $\log_b(MN) = \log_b M + \log_b N$. The quotient law inherits subtraction the same way, and $(b^m)^k = b^{mk}$ becomes the **power rule** $\log_b(M^k) = k\log_b M$ — exponents inside become coefficients outside. Nothing new was created; notation changed clothes.

steps.title: Which splits?
steps.explanation: Product inside → sum outside: $\log_2 8 + \log_2 x = 3 + \log_2 x$. Sums inside don't split; powers pull out as coefficients (not exponents on the log); ratios of logs are change-of-base, not quotient-rule.

steps.title: Back to one log
steps.explanation: Power rule first: $2\log_3 x = \log_3 x^2$; then product: $\log_3(4x^2)$. Coefficients must become exponents BEFORE combining.

steps.title: Any log, any base
steps.body: $\log_b x = \frac{\log_c x}{\log_c b}$: measure both $x$ and $b$ in a convenient base $c$, take the ratio. It's how calculators with only base-10 buttons compute $\log_7 50$, and how ugly logs simplify by hand: $\log_8 32$ through base 2 is $\frac{5}{3}$ — both 8 and 32 are powers of 2, and the formula sees it.

steps.title: Power rule first, both directions
steps.body: Expanding: strip the outside structure first (quotient, then products), pull powers last. Condensing: REVERSE — convert every coefficient into an exponent first, THEN merge with product/quotient rules. Merging before converting is the source of nearly every condensing error.

steps.title: What sticks
points: Product adds, quotient subtracts, power pulls to the front.
No law for sums inside; ratio of logs = change of base.

lessons.title: Solving Exponential & Log Equations
lessons.concreteComparison: "How long until my investment doubles?" is an equation with the unknown trapped in the exponent. Logs are the crowbar built for exactly that — and the famous Rule of 72 is this lesson's computation, memorized by bankers.
lessons.objective: Solve exponential equations (matching bases or taking logs) and log equations (condensing and unwrapping), checking domains throughout.
concept: **Matching bases**: when both sides are powers of one base, equate exponents. $8^{x} = 32$: $2^{3x} = 2^5 \to 3x = 5 \to x = \frac{5}{3}$. One-to-one functions permit exactly this move.

**Mismatched bases**: take a log of both sides and pull the exponent down with the power rule. $3^x = 20$: $x \log 3 = \log 20 \to x = \frac{\log 20}{\log 3} \approx 2.73$. Exact answer: the log expression; decimal: the calculator's business.

**Log equations**: condense each side to a single log, then either equate insides ($\log_2 A = \log_2 B \to A = B$) or unwrap to exponential form. DOMAIN CHECK is mandatory: candidates making any original log's inside $\le 0$ are extraneous — same phantom mechanics as radical equations.
lessons.keyIdea: Same base → equate exponents; different bases → log both sides and pull the power down; log equations condense then unwrap — and every candidate must keep all log insides positive.

facts.title: The crowbar
facts.explanation: Log both sides; power rule frees the unknown.

facts.title: Domain audit
facts.explanation: Check candidates against the ORIGINAL equation's logs.

workedExamples.solution: Isolate: $2^{3t} = 16 = 2^4 \to 3t = 4 \to t = \frac{4}{3}$. (Matching bases made logs unnecessary — always check for that first.)
Condense: $\log_2\big(x(x-6)\big) = 4 \to x^2 - 6x = 16 \to (x-8)(x+2) = 0 \to x = 8, -2$. Domain audit: $x = 8$: both insides positive ✓. $x = -2$: $\log_2(-2)$ doesn't exist ✗. Answer: $x = 8$.

commonMistakes.text: From $\log x + \log(x-6) = 4$ jumping to $x + (x - 6) = 10^4$... or worse, $= 4$.
commonMistakes.correction: Condense FIRST (sum of logs = log of product), then unwrap the single log: $x(x-6) = 10^4$ (base 10) — insides multiply, never add.

commonMistakes.text: Keeping $x = -2$ because it solves the quadratic.
commonMistakes.correction: The quadratic is the FORGETFUL version (like the squared radical equation). The original contains $\log_2 x$, and $\log_2(-2)$ doesn't exist. Audit every candidate against the original's domains.

tryIt.solution: Condense: $\log_3\frac{x+2}{x-4} = 2 \to \frac{x+2}{x-4} = 9 \to x + 2 = 9x - 36 \to x = \frac{38}{8} = \frac{19}{4}$. Audit: $x + 2 > 0$ ✓ and $x - 4 = \frac{3}{4} > 0$ ✓. Valid.

steps.title: The unknown upstairs
steps.body: 'When does $500 \cdot 2^t$ reach a million?' — the $t$ lives in the exponent, untouchable by add, subtract, multiply, divide, or root. This is the lesson the whole unit has aimed at: two families of equations, one crowbar (the log), and the discipline of the domain audit.

steps.title: Solving = finding the crossing
steps.teach: Every exponential equation is geometrically this picture: a growth curve, a target height, one crossing. The crossing EXISTS and is unique (exponentials never repeat a value) — the algebra below just names its coordinate exactly.

steps.title: Match bases, or log both sides
steps.body: Path 1 — both sides expressible in ONE base: rewrite and equate exponents ($8^x = 32 \to 2^{3x} = 2^5$). Path 2 — no common base ($3^x = 20$): take log of both sides; the power rule drags $x$ downstairs: $x = \frac{\log 20}{\log 3}$. Leave THAT as the exact answer; decimals are a calculator's afterthought.

steps.title: Free the x
steps.explanation: $\log(7^x) = x\log 7 = \log 45$: divide. (Answer B is the quotient rule misfiring — that would be $\log \frac{45}{7}$'s expansion, a different object.)

steps.title: Phantom hunt
steps.prompt: Solving $\log_5(x) + \log_5(x + 4) = 1$ gives candidates $x = 1$ and $x = -5$. The solution set is:
steps.explanation: $x(x+4) = 5$ has both roots, but the ORIGINAL demands $x > 0$ and $x + 4 > 0$: only $x = 1$ passes. Log equations inherit radical equations' phantom discipline.

steps.title: Doubling time, visualized
steps.teach: Compound growth: each bar is the last times the same factor. 'When does the balance double?' is $1.08^t = 2$ — by the crowbar, $t = \frac{\log 2}{\log 1.08} \approx 9$ years. (Bankers memorize this as the Rule of 72: $72 \div 8 \approx 9$.)

steps.title: Exact, then approximate
steps.body: The exact answer to $3^x = 20$ is $\frac{\log 20}{\log 3}$ — a complete, correct, checkable object. Write it BEFORE reaching for decimals. Exact answers compose (they plug into later steps losslessly); rounded ones accumulate drift.

steps.title: What sticks
points: Same base: equate exponents. Different: log both sides, power rule.
Log equations: condense to ONE log, unwrap, then domain-audit.

practice.statement: A population of 800 grows 10% yearly. Write the model and evaluate at $t = 2$.
Convert $\log_6 36 = 2$ to exponential form and $2^{10} = 1024$ to log form.
Condense $\log 7 + 2\log x - \log y$ into one log.

testYourself.statement: A $9000 machine depreciates 20% yearly. Model and value after 3 years?

testYourself.solution: $x^2 - 9 = 16 \to x = \pm 5$. Audit: $x = 5$ ✓; $x = -5$ makes both insides negative ✗. Answer 5.

testYourself.statement: An investment grows at 6% per year. Write the exact doubling time.
